title: Two-Port Network Parameter Representation (Z-Parameters)
description: An equivalent circuit diagram modeling Z-parameters (impedance parameters) of a two-port network, showing input/output loop variables V1, I1, V2, I2, self impedances Z11, Z22, and dependent voltage sources representing cross coupling.
aliases: Two-Port Network Parameter Representation (Z-Parameters) (Sem III - ENA)
theory:
A two-port network is an electrical network with two separate pairs of terminals (ports) for input and output, commonly analyzed in circuit theory and filter design. The Z-parameters, also called impedance parameters or open-circuit impedance parameters, relate the port voltages (V1, V2) directly to the port currents (I1, I2) through a system of linear equations: V1 = Z11 * I1 + Z12 * I2 and V2 = Z21 * I1 + Z22 * I2. In this description, Z11 is the input impedance with the output open-circuited (I2 = 0). Z12 is the reverse transfer impedance with the input open-circuited (I1 = 0). Z21 is the forward transfer impedance with the output open-circuited (I2 = 0). Z22 is the output impedance with the input open-circuited (I1 = 0).

The equivalent circuit representing these equations utilizes two independent loops. The input loop consists of a series impedance Z11 and a dependent voltage source Z12 * I2 controlled by the output port current I2. The output loop contains a series impedance Z22 and a dependent voltage source Z21 * I1 controlled by the input port current I1. If the network is reciprocal, then Z12 = Z21. If the network is symmetric, then Z11 = Z22. This model is exceptionally useful when two-port networks are connected in series, as the combined Z-parameters are simply the matrix sum of the individual networks' Z-parameters.
keyPoints: Z-parameters relate port voltages to port currents (V = Z * I).
Also called open-circuit parameters because they are determined by leaving one port open-circuited (I = 0).
Z11 is the input impedance, Z22 is the output impedance, Z12 and Z21 are transfer impedances.
The equivalent circuit uses dependent voltage sources (Z12*I2 and Z21*I1) to represent port coupling.
For reciprocal networks: Z12 = Z21. For symmetric networks: Z11 = Z22.
Z-parameter matrices can be directly added when networks are connected in series.
examTip: To find Z11 and Z21, open-circuit Port 2 (set I2 = 0) and compute V1/I1 and V2/I1 respectively. To find Z22 and Z12, open-circuit Port 1 (set I1 = 0) and compute V2/I2 and V1/I2. Remember: V1 = Z11*I1 + Z12*I2, and V2 = Z21*I1 + Z22*I2.
useCases: Analyzing transistor amplifier behavior using small-signal models (e.g., hybrid-pi conversion).
Designing and modeling transmission lines and electrical power grids.
Characterizing passive filter networks in communications systems.
Simplifying the analysis of series-connected multi-stage networks.
Performing RF and microwave circuit simulations using impedance parameters.

wires.comment: Port 1 top terminal to Z11 left
Z11 right to V1_dep top node
Connect to dependent source V1_dep top
Port 1 bottom terminal to bottom rail
Port 2 top terminal to Z22 right
Z22 left to V2_dep top node
Connect to dependent source V2_dep top
Port 2 bottom terminal to bottom rail
Common ground reference wire connecting both loops
